data_in.description: Aligned feature table from sample alignment step (containing m/z, retention time, and peak intensities across samples)

data_out.description: Grouped feature table with isotopologue and adduct cluster assignments for each detected feature

domain_knowledge: Isotopologue grouping relies on precise mass differences corresponding to stable isotopes: C13 (~1.003 Da), N15 (~0.997 Da), O18 (~2.004 Da), and D (~1.006 Da).

Common adduct masses must account for ionization mode: [M+H]+ (1.008 Da), [M+Na]+ (22.989 Da), [M+K]+ (38.963 Da), [M+NH4]+ (18.034 Da) in positive mode; [M−H]− (−1.008 Da), [M+Cl]− (34.969 Da) in negative mode.
Retention time clustering is essential to group features of the same molecule across adduct variants, as they elute at the same or very similar times due to identical chromatographic behavior.
Mass accuracy tolerance (typically ≤5 ppm) determines the allowable deviation from theoretical mass differences when linking isotopologues and adducts.

direct_checks: verify that the GitHub repository adelabriere/SLAW or zamboni-lab/SLAW contains a README or documentation file describing the post-picking grouping step
verify that the repository contains executable code or a module that implements isotopologue and adduct grouping
verify that example grouped feature output (table or file) exists in the repository, deposited dataset, or supplementary materials with at least one field representing feature groups
file_exists: grouped feature output file in repository or supplementary materials
field_present: grouped feature output contains identifiable fields for isotopologue group ID and/or adduct annotation
script_runs: grouping module executes without error on provided test LC-MS feature input
expert_review: verify that the implemented grouping algorithm correctly clusters detected features by isotopologue series (e.g., M, M+1, M+2 patterns)
verify that the grouping algorithm correctly identifies and separates adduct forms (e.g., [M+H]+, [M+Na]+, [M-H]−)
verify that grouped output is biologically and chemically sensible: isotopologue groups show expected mass deltas and adduct groups show expected mass shifts

finding: SLAW includes a processing step for grouping of isotopologues and adducts as part of its complete untargeted LC-MS workflow, which operates after peak picking and sample alignment.
inputs: Aligned feature table from sample alignment step (containing m/z, retention time, and peak intensities across samples)
methodology_summary: Load aligned feature table with m/z, retention time, and intensity data.
Define isotopologue mass differences (C13, N15, O18, D) and adduct mass transformations for the ionization mode.
Cluster features by binning on retention time windows and linking features within mass tolerance of theoretical isotopologue/adduct masses.
Assign group identifiers to isotopologues and adducts sharing the same molecular ion.

Validation: grouped feature table contains all input features, each assigned a unique or shared group ID; mass differences between clustered features match theoretical isotopologue or adduct masses within specified ppm tolerance.

task_description: Reconstruct the post-picking grouping module that clusters aligned LC-MS features into isotopologue and adduct groups. Produce a grouped feature table with cluster assignments.

task_inputs.label: Aligned feature table from sample alignment

task_objective: Identify and document the isotopologue and adduct grouping mechanism in SLAW, specifying its input features, grouping rules, and consolidated output format.

task_outputs.label: Grouped feature table with isotopologue and adduct cluster assignments

title: Reconstruct the Gap-Filling by Data Recursion Component
uncertainty_notes: This card was generated by the LLM-assisted pipeline and needs scientific expert review.
Each TracedClaim's evidence_span has been substring-checked against its source section; see logs/llm_calls.jsonl and capsules/<task_id>/quality_report.json for groundedness results.
workflow_description: 1. Load aligned feature table (output from sample alignment step) containing detected m/z and retention time coordinates. 2. Apply isotopologue and adduct grouping algorithm to cluster features sharing the same molecular ion with mass differences corresponding to isotopic shifts (e.g., C13, N15, D) or common adduct transformations (e.g., [M+H]+, [M+Na]+, [M+NH4]+). 3. Assign cluster identifiers to features and output grouped feature table with isotopologue/adduct group membership.
